text: {"analysis_summary": "Inspected the visible RTL under the input scope for permission/security controls around debug access, secure-mode signaling, and APB-programmable control registers. The strongest issue is an exposed APB-to-core debug path that reaches the core debug unit without any visible authentication, lock, privilege, or lifecycle enable check. The debug unit itself provides always-accessible control registers that can request halt and, once halted, can access CSRs, GPRs, and next-PC/jump controls. A second issue is that secure interrupt-related signals exist but are tied off or left unconnected in the FC integration, and the interrupt controller appears to allow APB writes to interrupt mask/status/ack registers without using its secure-mode input. There is also a lower-confidence warning that `supervisor_mode_o` is hardwired high, which could defeat downstream permission checks if that signal is used as a privilege qualifier outside this scope.", "findings": [{"finding_id": "PERM-DBG-001", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "APB debug slave can reach powerful core debug controls without visible permission or authentication checks.", "vulnerability_category": "Missing authorization / unrestricted debug access", "affected_locations": [{"file": "ips/pulp_soc/rtl/fc/fc_subsystem.sv", "line_start": 32, "line_end": 32, "module": "fc_subsystem", "signal_or_register": "apb_slave_debug"}, {"file": "ips/pulp_soc/rtl/fc/fc_subsystem.sv", "line_start": 297, "line_end": 319, "module": "fc_subsystem", "signal_or_register": "debug_req/debug_addr/debug_we/debug_wdata/debug_rdata"}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv", "line_start": 163, "line_end": 211, "module": "zeroriscy_debug_unit", "signal_or_register": "DBG_CTRL / debug register decode / GPR write path"}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv", "line_start": 456, "line_end": 467, "module": "zeroriscy_debug_unit", "signal_or_register": "regfile_rreq_o/regfile_wreq_o/csr_req_o/jump_req_o"}], "evidence": [{"file": "ips/pulp_soc/rtl/fc/fc_subsystem.sv", "line_start": 32, "line_end": 32, "module": "fc_subsystem", "object": "apb_slave_debug", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "The FC subsystem exposes a dedicated APB slave port for debug access.", "supports_claim": "Shows a bus-facing debug slave interface exists at the subsystem boundary."}, {"file": "ips/pulp_soc/rtl/fc/fc_subsystem.sv", "line_start": 297, "line_end": 319, "module": "fc_subsystem", "object": "apb2per_debug_i", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "The APB debug slave is directly connected through `apb2per_debug_i` to internal debug request, address, write, write-data, grant, rvalid, and read-data signals.", "supports_claim": "Shows APB transactions are converted into core debug-unit transactions without visible permission gating in this integration."}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv", "line_start": 163, "line_end": 163, "module": "zeroriscy_debug_unit", "object": "6'b00_0000 decode", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "The Zero-Riscy debug unit declares debug registers as 'always accessible'.", "supports_claim": "Indicates some debug control registers do not require the core to already be halted or authorized."}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv", "line_start": 167, "line_end": 172, "module": "zeroriscy_debug_unit", "object": "DBG_CTRL/dbg_halt", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "Writing DBG_CTRL can assert `dbg_halt` when the core is not halted.", "supports_claim": "Shows a bus-visible debug write can request halting the core."}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv", "line_start": 196, "line_end": 211, "module": "zeroriscy_debug_unit", "object": "DNPC/regfile_wreq", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "Once `debug_halted_o` is true, debug writes can request a next-PC jump and write general-purpose registers.", "supports_claim": "Shows the debug interface can alter execution state and register state after halt."}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_debug_unit.sv", "line_start": 456, "line_end": 467, "module": "zeroriscy_debug_unit", "object": "regfile_rreq_o/regfile_wreq_o/csr_req_o/jump_req_o", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "The debug unit outputs register-file read/write, CSR request, and jump request signals directly from its decoded internal requests.", "supports_claim": "Shows the debug transaction decode drives privileged internal control paths."}, {"file": ".", "line_start": null, "line_end": null, "module": "global", "object": "auth/debug_en/dbg_en", "evidence_type": "search_result", "description": "Searches for authentication or debug enable terms did not reveal `auth`, `debug_en`, or `dbg_en` controls in the visible source.", "supports_claim": "Supports that no visible authorization or enable gate protects the debug path within the inspected scope."}], "reasoning_summary": "The subsystem accepts APB debug transactions and converts them to the core debug interface. The debug unit has always-accessible control registers, including DBG_CTRL, which can request a halt. After the core is halted, the same interface can read/write GPRs and CSRs and request a PC jump. No visible authentication, privilege check, lifecycle lock, fuse enable, or secure-mode condition gates this path in the inspected RTL. Therefore, any master that can access `apb_slave_debug` can potentially take over the core through the debug interface.", "security_impact": "An unauthorized APB master could halt the processor, inspect or modify general-purpose registers and CSRs, and redirect execution. This can lead to full privilege escalation, secret extraction, firmware tampering, denial of service, and bypass of software privilege boundaries.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The inspected scope does not include the full top-level APB interconnect or any external security firewall. If an enclosing module permanently blocks untrusted access to `apb_slave_debug`, exploitability may be reduced. However, no such protection is visible in the provided source scope.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Add an explicit debug authorization/lifecycle enable signal and gate all debug APB requests before they reach the core debug unit.", "Ensure debug access is disabled or locked in production unless authenticated.", "Return APB errors or block grants for unauthorized debug transactions instead of allowing the debug unit to grant invalid or unauthorized requests.", "Verify at the SoC address-map/firewall level that `apb_slave_debug` is inaccessible to untrusted software or external masters."]}, {"finding_id": "PERM-IRQ-002", "status": "potential_warning", "summary": "Secure interrupt controls appear tied off or unused, while APB can directly modify interrupt control state.", "vulnerability_category": "Missing secure-mode/privilege enforcement on control registers", "affected_locations": [{"file": "ips/apb_interrupt_cntrl/apb_interrupt_cntrl.sv", "line_start": 26, "line_end": 47, "module": "apb_interrupt_cntrl", "signal_or_register": "ENA_SEC_IRQ/core_irq_sec_o/core_secure_mode_i"}, {"file": "ips/apb_interrupt_cntrl/apb_interrupt_cntrl.sv", "line_start": 134, "line_end": 162, "module": "apb_interrupt_cntrl", "signal_or_register": "r_mask/r_int APB write paths"}, {"file": "ips/pulp_soc/rtl/fc/fc_subsystem.sv", "line_start": 275, "line_end": 288, "module": "fc_subsystem", "signal_or_register": "fc_eu_i core_secure_mode_i/core_irq_sec_o"}], "evidence": [{"file": "ips/apb_interrupt_cntrl/apb_interrupt_cntrl.sv", "line_start": 26, "line_end": 47, "module": "apb_interrupt_cntrl", "object": "ENA_SEC_IRQ/core_irq_sec_o/core_secure_mode_i", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "The interrupt controller has a secure interrupt parameter and secure-mode-related ports.", "supports_claim": "Shows the design intends to carry secure interrupt/security context information."}, {"file": "ips/apb_interrupt_cntrl/apb_interrupt_cntrl.sv", "line_start": 134, "line_end": 134, "module": "apb_interrupt_cntrl", "object": "s_mask_next", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "APB writes can directly replace or modify the interrupt mask register.", "supports_claim": "Shows APB software can control interrupt masking without a visible secure-mode/permission condition."}, {"file": "ips/apb_interrupt_cntrl/apb_interrupt_cntrl.sv", "line_start": 162, "line_end": 162, "module": "apb_interrupt_cntrl", "object": "s_int_next", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "APB writes can directly replace or modify pending interrupt state.", "supports_claim": "Shows APB software can inject or clear interrupt state without a visible secure-mode/permission condition."}, {"file": "ips/pulp_soc/rtl/fc/fc_subsystem.sv", "line_start": 283, "line_end": 288, "module": "fc_subsystem", "object": "core_secure_mode_i/core_irq_sec_o", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "The FC subsystem ties the interrupt controller secure-mode input low and leaves the secure IRQ output unconnected.", "supports_claim": "Shows secure interrupt signaling is disabled or ignored in the integration."}, {"file": "ips/apb_interrupt_cntrl/apb_interrupt_cntrl.sv", "line_start": null, "line_end": null, "module": "apb_interrupt_cntrl", "object": "core_irq_sec_o/core_secure_mode_i/ENA_SEC_IRQ", "evidence_type": "search_result", "description": "Search for `core_irq_sec`, `core_secure_mode`, and `ENA_SEC_IRQ` found declarations/integration but no visible assignment/use of `core_irq_sec_o` in the interrupt controller beyond its port declaration.", "supports_claim": "Supports that the secure interrupt feature is not implemented or not active in the visible source."}], "reasoning_summary": "The interrupt controller exposes secure interrupt-related parameters and ports, but the FC subsystem ties `core_secure_mode_i` to `1'b0` and discards `core_irq_sec_o`. Within the controller, APB writes directly alter interrupt mask and pending state, and the visible logic does not condition those writes on secure mode or privilege. This creates a permission concern for interrupt control: untrusted APB-accessible software or masters may be able to mask, acknowledge, inject, or clear interrupts, including security-sensitive events, without secure-mode enforcement.", "security_impact": "If untrusted software or bus masters can access this APB slave, they may disable or spoof interrupts, hide security events, force unexpected control flow, or cause denial of service. Tying off secure-mode handling may also cause secure and non-secure interrupt domains to be indistinguishable.", "confidence": "medium", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The provided scope does not show the full SoC bus protection policy, so APB access may be externally restricted. It is also unclear whether this design intentionally does not implement secure interrupt domains despite carrying secure-related ports.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Implement and verify secure interrupt attribution using `ENA_SEC_IRQ`, `core_secure_mode_i`, and `core_irq_sec_o`, or remove the unused security ports to avoid false assumptions.", "Gate APB writes to interrupt mask/pending/ack registers based on privilege or secure context where required.", "Add APB error/deny behavior for unauthorized writes to interrupt-control registers.", "Review the full SoC APB address map/firewall to confirm only trusted privileged masters can access the event/interrupt unit."]}, {"finding_id": "PERM-MODE-003", "status": "potential_warning", "summary": "`supervisor_mode_o` is hardwired privileged/high, which may defeat downstream permission checks if used as an access qualifier.", "vulnerability_category": "Incorrect privilege-mode signaling", "affected_locations": [{"file": "ips/pulp_soc/rtl/fc/fc_subsystem.sv", "line_start": 44, "line_end": 273, "module": "fc_subsystem", "signal_or_register": "supervisor_mode_o"}], "evidence": [{"file": "ips/pulp_soc/rtl/fc/fc_subsystem.sv", "line_start": 44, "line_end": 44, "module": "fc_subsystem", "object": "supervisor_mode_o", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "The subsystem exposes a `supervisor_mode_o` output.", "supports_claim": "Shows the FC subsystem exports a privilege/permission-related mode signal."}, {"file": "ips/pulp_soc/rtl/fc/fc_subsystem.sv", "line_start": 273, "line_end": 273, "module": "fc_subsystem", "object": "assign supervisor_mode_o = 1'b1", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "The exported supervisor-mode signal is hardwired high.", "supports_claim": "Shows the signal does not reflect dynamic processor privilege state."}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv", "line_start": 163, "line_end": 163, "module": "zeroriscy_cs_registers", "object": "mstatus", "evidence_type": "source_line", "description": "The Zero-Riscy CSR implementation comments that `mstatus` is always M-mode.", "supports_claim": "Supports that dynamic lower-privilege modes are not visibly implemented in this core path."}], "reasoning_summary": "`supervisor_mode_o` is a permission-relevant output, but the FC subsystem drives it constantly to `1'b1`. If downstream memory, peripheral, or interconnect logic uses this signal to distinguish privileged from unprivileged accesses, all FC accesses would be marked privileged/supervisor, bypassing permission checks. The visible scope does not show the consumers of this signal, so this is reported as a low-confidence warning rather than a confirmed exploit.", "security_impact": "Potential privilege-tag spoofing: downstream components may treat all accesses as privileged, allowing untrusted firmware or tasks to access protected resources.", "confidence": "low", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The inspected files do not include consumers of `supervisor_mode_o`; the signal may be unused or only informational outside this scope. The core may also intentionally operate only in machine mode.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Trace all consumers of `supervisor_mode_o` in the full SoC and verify it is not used to grant privileged memory/peripheral access.", "If privilege separation is required, drive this signal from actual CPU privilege state instead of a constant.", "If the core intentionally only supports machine/supervisor operation, document that assumption and ensure no untrusted code executes on this core."]}], "no_finding_reason": "", "global_uncertainty": "Analysis is limited to the provided source files and search/read outputs. The complete SoC top-level, APB interconnect, address firewall, lifecycle/debug-lock logic, and software access policy are not visible. Findings describe missing or bypassed permission enforcement in the inspected RTL; exploitability depends on whether external modules restrict access to the affected APB slaves and debug interfaces."}
